module.label: Pediatric chest pain, syncope, or palpitations

use_when: Child or adolescent with chest pain, exertional symptoms, syncope, near-syncope, palpitations, or concern for pediatric cardiac or respiratory chest pain.

Pediatric chest-pain or fainting workup needs age-band routing, cardiac red-flag screening, ECG interpretation with pediatric limits, respiratory/PE/myocarditis/pericarditis screening, and source-backed testing restraint.
do_not_use_when: Adult patient; use adult ACS, PE, arrhythmia, syncope, or dyspnea pathway as appropriate.

Unstable child with shock, cyanosis, persistent altered mental status, sustained tachyarrhythmia, severe respiratory distress, or active resuscitation needs; escalate immediately while documenting focused findings.
Primary respiratory distress or wheeze without chest pain, syncope, or palpitations; use pediatric respiratory distress, wheeze, or bronchiolitis pathway.
Pregnant or recently postpartum adolescent with chest pain, dyspnea, syncope, or VTE concern; add pregnancy/postpartum maternal warning and PE/OB escalation guidance.

differentialBuckets.label: Cardiac chest pain or syncope risk with exertional symptoms, syncope, palpitations, abnormal cardiac exam, abnormal ECG, cardiac history, or family history of early sudden death
differentialBuckets.action: Screen cardiac red flags before reassurance; obtain ECG and involve senior clinician/cardiology when red flags, abnormal exam, or abnormal ECG are present.
differentialBuckets.rationale: Serious cardiac causes are uncommon in pediatric chest pain, but exertional pain/syncope, palpitations, abnormal exam, abnormal ECG, and family history change urgency.

source.source_section: risk factors for serious or life-threatening chest pain and investigation guidance
source.implementation_notes: Use pediatric ECG interpretation and senior/cardiology review rather than adult ACS scores as the default frame.

differentialBuckets.diagnostic_target: Pediatric cardiac chest pain, cardiogenic syncope, arrhythmia, myocarditis/pericarditis, cardiomyopathy, congenital/acquired heart disease, or inherited arrhythmia syndrome.
differentialBuckets.management_change: Changes ECG urgency, monitoring, cardiology consultation, echocardiography/Holter/exercise-test consideration, activity restriction, transfer, and discharge eligibility.
differentialBuckets.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not assigned to this broad cardiac-risk frame; source-backed red flags and ECG findings drive management.
differentialBuckets.limitations: A normal ECG does not erase exertional syncope, family history, or abnormal exam concern; discuss red-flag presentations even if ECG appears normal.

differentialBuckets.label: Respiratory, pulmonary embolus, pneumothorax, asthma, pneumonia, or hypoxemic cause of pediatric chest pain
differentialBuckets.action: Ask pleuritic pain, dyspnea, cough, fever, hemoptysis, hypoxia, trauma, chronic respiratory disease, and PE risk factors before choosing CXR, respiratory testing, or PE pathway.
differentialBuckets.rationale: Pediatric chest pain with dyspnea, hypoxia, pleuritic features, fever/cough, hemoptysis, trauma, or PE risks changes imaging, oxygen, and escalation.

source.source_section: pulmonary embolus, respiratory, and chest pain risk factors
source.implementation_notes: Use pediatric respiratory pathway when respiratory distress dominates; this row is for chest-pain presentations needing respiratory danger screening.

differentialBuckets.diagnostic_target: Pneumonia, asthma, pneumothorax, pulmonary embolus, hypoxemia, trauma-related chest injury, or other respiratory cause of chest pain.
differentialBuckets.management_change: Changes oxygen, CXR, respiratory testing, PE/DVT add-on, isolation, antibiotics, chest drain/escalation, and disposition.
differentialBuckets.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not attached to this broad respiratory frame; source-specific testing is triggered by symptoms and exam.
differentialBuckets.limitations: Do not use this row to rule out PE or pneumothorax when hypoxia, hemoptysis, pleuritic pain, trauma, or asymmetric breath sounds are present.

differentialBuckets.label: Musculoskeletal pain, costochondritis, precordial catch, anxiety, reflux, or other self-limited cause after serious features are absent
differentialBuckets.action: Document reproducible localized pain, brief sharp episodes, recent overuse/trauma/cough, normal cardiopulmonary exam, and absence of red flags before reassurance or simple analgesia.
differentialBuckets.rationale: Most pediatric chest pain is self-limited, but reassurance is safe only after red flags and concerning exam features are actively screened.

source.source_section: key points, musculoskeletal causes, precordial catch, and reassurance guidance
source.implementation_notes: Avoid unnecessary ECG/CXR/labs when low-risk pattern is clear and follow-up/safety-netting is appropriate.

differentialBuckets.diagnostic_target: Low-risk pediatric chest pain such as musculoskeletal pain, costochondritis, precordial catch, anxiety, or reflux.
differentialBuckets.management_change: Changes from broad testing to reassurance, analgesia, return precautions, and follow-up only when red flags are absent.
differentialBuckets.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not assigned to this low-risk frame; use active denial of red flags and focused exam findings.
differentialBuckets.limitations: Do not apply low-risk disposition when exertional symptoms, syncope, palpitations, abnormal vitals, abnormal exam, concerning ECG, or serious family history is present.

differentialBuckets.label: Vasovagal, orthostatic, POTS, seizure mimic, hypoglycemia, toxin, or cardiogenic syncope pattern
differentialBuckets.action: Characterize event position, trigger, prodrome, exertion, palpitations/chest pain, duration, recovery, incontinence, movements, family history, and orthostatic measurements.
differentialBuckets.rationale: Syncope history separates common autonomic causes from cardiac syncope, seizure mimic, hypoglycemia, and toxin exposure that change testing and referral.

source.source_section: syncope causes, history features, examination, investigations, consultation, transfer, and discharge
source.implementation_notes: Obtain ECG at least once for syncope unless previously done and no new concern; use BGL shortly after event when indicated.

differentialBuckets.diagnostic_target: Vasovagal syncope, orthostatic hypotension, POTS, cardiogenic syncope, seizure mimic, hypoglycemia, toxin exposure, or functional disorder.
differentialBuckets.management_change: Changes ECG, orthostatic measurements, bedside glucose, anemia/pregnancy testing, cardiology/neurology referral, transfer, and discharge education.
differentialBuckets.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not assigned to this broad syncope frame; guideline-based event features and ECG/exam findings drive routing.
differentialBuckets.limitations: Do not label exertional syncope or syncope with chest pain/palpitations as vasovagal without cardiac review.

safetyChecks.label: Measure blood pressure
safetyChecks.action: Measure blood pressure with an age-appropriate cuff and document repeat value if hypotension, hypertension, pain, or perfusion concern is present.
safetyChecks.rationale: Hypotension, hypertension, or perfusion concern changes shock, myocarditis/pericarditis, arrhythmia, PE, sepsis, and transfer decisions.

source.source_section: risk factors for cardiovascular etiology: hypotension and exam findings
source.implementation_notes: Interpret with age-specific norms and the full cardiopulmonary exam.

safetyChecks.management_change: Abnormal blood pressure changes monitoring, senior/cardiology review, fluid/vasoactive planning, and transfer urgency.
safetyChecks.limitations: Wrong cuff size, anxiety, pain, movement, and recent activity can distort pediatric readings.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to blood pressure as baseline safety data; management is threshold- and context-driven.

safetyChecks.label: Measure heart rate
safetyChecks.action: Measure heart rate and document regularity, trend, pain/fever context, and whether palpitations are occurring during measurement.
safetyChecks.rationale: Tachycardia, bradycardia, irregular rhythm, or symptoms during measurement can signal arrhythmia, myocarditis, dehydration, fever, PE, or anxiety.

source.source_section: ECG rate and rhythm interpretation in clinical context
source.implementation_notes: Heart rate is baseline data; ECG rhythm interpretation is a diagnostic test, not a bedside maneuver.

safetyChecks.management_change: Abnormal rate or rhythm changes ECG urgency, monitoring, cardiology consultation, and disposition.
safetyChecks.limitations: Fever, anxiety, pain, dehydration, medications, and activity affect heart rate; interpret with ECG and symptoms.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to heart rate as baseline clinical data.

safetyChecks.label: Measure respiratory rate
safetyChecks.action: Count respiratory rate for a full minute when feasible and compare with age-specific concern thresholds.
safetyChecks.rationale: Tachypnea changes concern for pneumonia, asthma, pneumothorax, PE, sepsis, anxiety/hyperventilation, or metabolic compensation.

source.source_section: respiratory risk factors and assessment of pediatric chest pain
source.implementation_notes: Use pediatric age-specific interpretation and pair with oxygen saturation and work of breathing.

safetyChecks.management_change: Abnormal respiratory rate changes oxygen, CXR/respiratory testing, PE/pneumothorax consideration, and escalation.
safetyChecks.limitations: Pain, anxiety, crying, fever, and exertion affect rate; reassess after settling when safe.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to respiratory rate as baseline clinical data.

safetyChecks.label: Measure oxygen saturation
safetyChecks.action: Measure oxygen saturation on room air when safe and document oxygen device, probe fit, perfusion, motion, and trend.
safetyChecks.rationale: Hypoxemia changes respiratory, PE, pneumonia, pneumothorax, myocarditis, and transfer decisions.

source.source_section: respiratory and pulmonary embolus risk factors for pediatric chest pain
source.implementation_notes: Document room-air versus oxygen-supported values.

safetyChecks.management_change: Low oxygen saturation changes oxygen therapy, CXR/respiratory testing, PE/pneumothorax pathway, monitoring, and transfer.
safetyChecks.limitations: Motion, poor perfusion, wrong probe size, and skin-pigmentation/device limitations can affect readings.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to oxygen saturation as baseline safety data; management is threshold- and trajectory-driven.

safetyChecks.label: Measure temperature
safetyChecks.action: Measure temperature and document fever trajectory, antipyretic timing, recent infection, and vaccination context when relevant.
safetyChecks.rationale: Fever changes concern for pneumonia, myocarditis, pericarditis, sepsis, and post-infectious chest pain.

source.source_section: recent illness, vaccination, respiratory, and infectious risk factors
source.implementation_notes: Add pediatric fever/sepsis pathway when fever plus concerning physiology or unclear source is present.

safetyChecks.management_change: Fever changes respiratory testing, inflammatory labs/troponin consideration, sepsis screening, and disposition.
safetyChecks.limitations: Temperature alone does not diagnose myocarditis, pericarditis, pneumonia, or sepsis.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to temperature as baseline clinical data.

safetyChecks.label: Measure orthostatic blood pressure
safetyChecks.action: When syncope, presyncope, dizziness, or orthostatic intolerance is present, document supine and standing blood pressure and heart rate with symptoms.
safetyChecks.rationale: Orthostatic changes support vasovagal/orthostatic/POTS patterns and can reveal dehydration, anemia, medication effect, or unsafe discharge.

source.source_section: syncope examination and autonomic causes including orthostatic hypotension and POTS thresholds
source.implementation_notes: Use only when safe to stand; pair with ECG and cardiac red flags.

safetyChecks.management_change: Orthostatic hypotension or marked postural tachycardia changes fluids/salt advice, medication review, anemia/dehydration workup, follow-up, and discharge safety.
safetyChecks.limitations: Unsafe standing, acute illness, dehydration, medications, and timing affect interpretation; do not let orthostatic findings override cardiac red flags.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to orthostatic blood pressure as baseline safety data; RCH uses threshold and symptom patterns.

safetyChecks.label: Measure pain score
safetyChecks.action: Measure pain score, location, and whether pain is ongoing, worsening, exertional, pleuritic, positional, reproducible, or waking the child from sleep.
safetyChecks.rationale: Pain severity and pattern change cardiac/respiratory red flags, analgesia, testing, observation, and discharge safety.

source.source_section: history features and musculoskeletal/precordial catch characteristics
source.implementation_notes: Pain score is baseline symptom severity data, not a physical exam maneuver.

safetyChecks.management_change: Severe, persistent, exertional, pleuritic, or sleep-waking pain changes ECG/CXR/lab decisions, observation, and escalation.
safetyChecks.limitations: Pain scores vary by age, anxiety, communication, and development; pair with pattern and exam.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to pain score as baseline symptom-severity data.
findings_options: mild pain 0 to 3 or age-appropriate equivalent
moderate pain 4 to 6 or age-appropriate equivalent
severe pain 7 to 10 or age-appropriate equivalent
exertional, pleuritic, positional, or sleep-waking pain

requiredQuestions.label: Pediatric chest pain age band
requiredQuestions.text: Which age band applies: under 6 weeks, 6 weeks to under 3 months, 3 to 11 months, 12 to 23 months, 2 to 6 years, 7 to 11 years, 12 to 15 years, or 16 to 21 years?
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask in every pediatric chest pain, syncope, or palpitations workup.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Age changes likelihood of benign chest pain, syncope causes, congenital disease, myocarditis/pericarditis, pregnancy applicability, and pathway scope.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Age band changes ECG interpretation limits, adolescent confidential history, pregnancy testing, sports/activity counseling, and adult pathway exclusion.

source.source_section: scope ages 7 to 21 years and pediatric chest pain pathway rationale
source.implementation_notes: Use local pediatric/neonatal pathways for infants; this module still blocks adult ACS assumptions for all pediatric-age contexts.

requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not applicable to age-band eligibility; age determines pathway scope and ECG interpretation.

requiredQuestions.label: Chest pain character and timing
requiredQuestions.text: Did the chest pain start acutely, occur with exertion, feel crushing or substernal, radiate to shoulder, arm, neck, jaw, or back, worsen with inspiration, or wake the child from sleep?
options: radiates to shoulder arm neck jaw or back
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask in every child or adolescent with chest pain.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies cardiac, respiratory, PE, pneumothorax, myocarditis/pericarditis, and lower-risk musculoskeletal patterns.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Positive serious features change ECG, monitoring, CXR/labs, cardiology/senior review, observation, and discharge restrictions.

source.source_section: risk factors for cardiovascular etiology: history features
source.implementation_notes: Document endorsed and denied features; do not collapse into adult ACS character alone.

requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for this feature cluster; source-backed risk features trigger ECG and cardiology/senior review.

requiredQuestions.label: Syncope palpitations and dizziness
requiredQuestions.text: Are syncope, near-syncope, dizziness, palpitations, poor exercise tolerance, or symptoms during exercise present?
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask in every chest pain, syncope, or palpitations presentation.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies cardiogenic syncope, arrhythmia, inherited arrhythmia syndrome, POTS/orthostatic intolerance, and seizure mimic.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Positive symptoms change ECG urgency, orthostatic measurements, bedside glucose, cardiology/senior review, transfer, and discharge restrictions.

source.source_section: history features differentiating autonomic syncope, cardiogenic syncope, and seizures
source.implementation_notes: Exertional syncope or syncope with chest pain/palpitations should not be treated as benign without cardiac review.

requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for this symptom cluster; event features and ECG/exam drive routing.

requiredQuestions.label: Family and personal cardiac risk
requiredQuestions.text: Is there congenital or acquired heart disease, Kawasaki disease, cardiomyopathy, pulmonary hypertension, aortopathy/connective tissue disease, Duchenne muscular dystrophy, sudden death, early myocardial infarction, arrhythmia, or unexplained sudden death in a young relative?
options: sudden death or early MI in family
family arrhythmia or unexplained sudden death
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask in every pediatric chest pain, syncope, palpitations, or exercise symptom presentation.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies inherited arrhythmia/cardiomyopathy, coronary aneurysm risk after Kawasaki disease, pulmonary hypertension, or structural/aortopathy risk.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Positive history changes ECG interpretation urgency, cardiology consultation, echo/Holter/exercise testing consideration, activity restriction, and family counseling.

source.source_section: past medical history and family history risk factors for cardiovascular etiology
source.implementation_notes: Ask family history directly; it is a pediatric cardiac red flag even with a normal first ECG.

requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for this risk-history cluster; guideline red flags change cardiology routing.

requiredQuestions.label: Respiratory and pulmonary embolus features
requiredQuestions.text: Are dyspnea, orthopnea, cough, fever, pleuritic pain, hemoptysis, hypoxia, chronic respiratory disease, recent surgery, immobility, hypercoagulability, central venous catheter, estrogen exposure, or prior PE/DVT present?
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask when chest pain, dyspnea, syncope, hypoxia, or pleuritic symptoms are present.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Screens for pneumonia, asthma, pneumothorax, PE, hypoxemia, and respiratory-source chest pain.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Positive features change oxygen, CXR, respiratory testing, PE/DVT add-on, antibiotics, monitoring, and transfer.

source.source_section: pulmonary embolus and respiratory risk factors
source.implementation_notes: Use pediatric respiratory or PE pathway when respiratory/PE concern is prominent.

requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for this respiratory/PE screen; source-backed features trigger focused testing and secondary workups.

requiredQuestions.label: Recent illness vaccination drug exposure
requiredQuestions.text: Has there been recent viral illness, fever, COVID-19 or mRNA vaccination, myocarditis/pericarditis concern, stimulant/cocaine/amphetamine use, QT-prolonging medication, or IV drug use?
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask when chest pain is persistent, substernal/crushing, pleuritic/positional, associated with fever, abnormal ECG, tachycardia, or adolescent confidential history is indicated.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies myocarditis/pericarditis, toxicologic arrhythmia risk, infectious endocarditis risk, and medication-related QT risk.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Positive features change ECG, troponin/inflammatory labs, blood culture when bacteremia risk exists, cardiology/senior review, and activity restriction.

source.source_section: recent illness/vaccination history and psychogenic/drug history considerations
source.implementation_notes: Use confidential adolescent history practices for drug and pregnancy-related contexts.

requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for this exposure screen; exposures trigger source-specific testing and safety decisions.

requiredQuestions.label: Reproducible musculoskeletal and GI features
requiredQuestions.text: Is pain localized, reproducible with palpation, movement, or deep breathing, associated with recent trauma or overuse, brief sharp precordial episodes, recurrent vomiting, odynophagia, dysphagia, food impaction, epigastric pain, or foreign-body/button-battery concern?
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask after serious cardiac/respiratory features are screened in every pediatric chest pain presentation.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies lower-risk musculoskeletal/precordial catch patterns and GI/foreign-body causes requiring different care.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Reproducible low-risk features can support analgesia/reassurance; GI foreign-body or button-battery concern changes urgent imaging/escalation.

source.source_section: musculoskeletal, precordial catch, gastrointestinal, and foreign body chest pain causes
source.implementation_notes: Do not let reproducibility override exertional syncope, abnormal vitals, abnormal ECG, or serious history.

requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for this lower-risk feature cluster; it changes testing restraint only when red flags are absent.

conditionalQuestions.label: Syncope event details
conditionalQuestions.text: For syncope or near-syncope, did it occur during exercise, without prodrome, after standing/heat/hunger/emotion, with chest pain or palpitations, with prolonged confusion, incontinence, tonic-clonic movements, or clustering?
options: standing heat hunger or emotion trigger

termsAny: after standing heat hunger or emotion

conditionalQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask when syncope, near-syncope, dizziness, collapse, or fainting is present.
conditionalQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Distinguishes autonomic/orthostatic syncope from cardiogenic syncope and seizure mimic.
conditionalQuestions.management_implication: Exercise syncope, no prodrome, chest pain/palpitations, clustering, or cardiac features changes ECG/cardiology/transfer; seizure features change neurology referral.

source.source_section: history features differentiating autonomic syncope, cardiogenic syncope, and seizure
source.implementation_notes: Use with orthostatic vitals and ECG; do not call exertional syncope benign without review.

conditionalQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for this event-detail cluster; guideline event features drive routing.

conditionalQuestions.label: Palpitations and arrhythmia details
conditionalQuestions.text: For palpitations, was onset sudden, was rhythm fast or irregular, did symptoms occur with exercise, chest pain, syncope, dizziness, stimulant use, or family arrhythmia/sudden death?
conditionalQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask when palpitations, heart racing, irregular rhythm, tachycardia, or arrhythmia concern is present.
conditionalQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies arrhythmia-risk patterns requiring ECG, monitoring, cardiology review, or Holter planning.
conditionalQuestions.management_implication: Positive features change ECG/monitoring urgency, cardiology consultation, activity restriction, medication/drug review, and disposition.

source.source_section: ECG indications, rhythm assessment, long QT, WPW, and cardiac red flags
source.implementation_notes: Manual interval review is needed when machine interpretation is questionable.

conditionalQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for this palpitations cluster; ECG and event features change management.

conditionalQuestions.label: Pulmonary embolus risk details
conditionalQuestions.text: If PE is possible, are prior PE/DVT, coagulopathy, estrogen therapy, pregnancy possibility, central venous catheter, recent surgery, immobility, hemoptysis, unilateral leg symptoms, or hypoxia present?
conditionalQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask when pleuritic chest pain, dyspnea, hypoxia, hemoptysis, syncope, or PE risk factors are present.
conditionalQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies when a validated PE/DVT workup should be added rather than relying on generic pediatric chest pain evaluation.
conditionalQuestions.management_implication: Positive features change PE/DVT secondary workup, D-dimer/imaging threshold, anticoagulation safety review, and transfer.

source.source_section: PE risk factors and D-dimer caution in pediatric chest pain
source.implementation_notes: Use a dedicated PE pathway when risk factors are present; D-dimer is nonspecific and must be interpreted in context.

conditionalQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for this PE screen; PE-specific pathway evidence should guide testing.

conditionalQuestions.label: Adolescent pregnancy context
conditionalQuestions.text: For a pregnancy-capable adolescent with chest pain, dyspnea, syncope, or PE risk, is pregnancy possible, currently pregnant, recently postpartum, using estrogen therapy, or having unilateral leg symptoms?
conditionalQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask confidentially when adolescent pregnancy/estrogen/postpartum context could alter PE, medication, or imaging decisions.
conditionalQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies pregnancy/postpartum or estrogen-related VTE and imaging/medication constraints.
conditionalQuestions.management_implication: Positive context changes PE/maternal warning add-on, pregnancy testing, imaging/medication choices, OB/MFM handoff, and discharge safety.

source.source_section: pulmonary embolus risk factors and adolescent psychosocial/drug history considerations
source.implementation_notes: Use local confidentiality policy and pregnancy/postpartum pathways when positive.

conditionalQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not applicable to pregnancy-context screening; it changes PE and imaging/medication safety.

requiredExam.label: Auscultate heart sounds
requiredExam.technique: Auscultate precordium for rhythm regularity, new or significant murmur, gallop, click, muffled heart sounds, and friction rub.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform in every pediatric chest pain, syncope, or palpitations workup.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Arrhythmia, structural heart disease, myocarditis, pericarditis, cardiomyopathy, or cardiac red flag.

source.source_section: physical exam risk factors for cardiovascular etiology
source.implementation_notes: Abnormal findings should prompt ECG/cardiology/senior review.

requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available for heart auscultation in this module; abnormal findings are source-backed cardiac red flags.
requiredExam.management_change: Abnormal sounds change ECG, monitoring, CXR/labs discussion, cardiology consultation, observation, and transfer urgency.
requiredExam.limitations: Murmur and rub detection depends on environment, positioning, and clinician experience; normal auscultation does not rule out arrhythmia or inherited risk.

requiredExam.label: Palpate peripheral pulses
requiredExam.technique: Palpate radial and femoral/peripheral pulses when feasible, comparing strength, symmetry, and perfusion context.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform in pediatric chest pain, syncope, or suspected cardiac/aortopathy presentations.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Poor perfusion, shock, congenital heart disease, aortopathy/coarctation concern, or severe cardiopulmonary illness.

source.source_section: physical exam cardiovascular risk factors including decreased femoral/peripheral pulses
source.implementation_notes: Pair with blood pressure and perfusion assessment.

requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available; pulse abnormalities are source-backed red flags requiring escalation.
requiredExam.management_change: Weak, asymmetric, or decreased femoral pulses change senior/cardiology review, imaging consideration, monitoring, and transfer.
requiredExam.limitations: Body habitus, movement, and distress can limit pulse comparison; do not delay escalation for perfect documentation.

requiredExam.label: Auscultate lung fields
requiredExam.technique: Auscultate anterior and posterior lung fields for wheeze, crackles, decreased or asymmetric breath sounds, and work-of-breathing context.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform when chest pain, dyspnea, cough, fever, hypoxia, trauma, or pneumothorax/pneumonia/asthma concern is present.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Pneumonia, asthma, pneumothorax, pleural disease, hypoxemic respiratory illness, or respiratory chest pain.

source.source_section: CXR indications including cough, shortness of breath, hypoxia, abnormal or asymmetric breath sounds, and trauma
source.implementation_notes: Abnormal lung findings trigger respiratory testing/CXR rather than routine broad cardiac labs.

requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available for this broad lung auscultation item; findings trigger source-specific respiratory pathways.
requiredExam.management_change: Abnormal lung findings change CXR, oxygen/bronchodilator/antibiotic consideration, respiratory pathway selection, and disposition.
requiredExam.limitations: Early pneumonia or small pneumothorax can have subtle findings; interpret with oxygen saturation and symptoms.

requiredExam.label: Observe work of breathing
requiredExam.technique: Observe speech, retractions, nasal flaring, accessory muscle use, tachypnea appearance, cyanosis, and ability to lie comfortably.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform when chest pain is associated with dyspnea, orthopnea, hypoxia, cough, fever, or respiratory distress.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Respiratory distress, hypoxemia, pneumonia, asthma, PE, pneumothorax, or cardiopulmonary compromise.

source.source_section: physical exam risk factors including cyanosis, tachypnea, shortness of breath, and work of breathing
source.implementation_notes: Use pediatric respiratory pathway when work of breathing is the dominant problem.

requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available; work-of-breathing abnormalities change acuity and respiratory routing.
requiredExam.management_change: Increased work of breathing changes oxygen, respiratory treatment, CXR/testing, monitoring, and transfer.
requiredExam.limitations: Anxiety and pain can mimic dyspnea; pair with oxygen saturation, lung exam, and trajectory.

requiredExam.label: Palpate chest wall
requiredExam.technique: Palpate the localized painful area and adjacent costochondral junctions while asking whether pain is reproduced by palpation.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform when musculoskeletal pain, costochondritis, trauma, overuse, cough-related pain, or low-risk chest pain is possible.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Musculoskeletal pain, costochondritis, trauma, or lower-risk chest wall pain pattern.

source.source_section: musculoskeletal chest pain and costochondritis features
source.implementation_notes: Reproducible tenderness supports musculoskeletal pattern only after cardiac/respiratory red flags are absent.

requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available for chest wall palpation; reproducibility supports low-risk pattern only in context.
requiredExam.management_change: Reproducible localized tenderness can support analgesia/reassurance and testing restraint when red flags are absent.
requiredExam.limitations: Reproducibility does not override exertional symptoms, syncope, abnormal vitals, abnormal ECG, or serious family history.

conditionalExam.label: Inspect skin perfusion
conditionalExam.technique: Inspect central color, pallor, mottling, sweatiness, and peripheral perfusion while correlating with pulse and blood pressure.
conditionalExam.when_to_perform: Perform when cyanosis, hypotension, shock, arrhythmia, myocarditis/pericarditis, PE, or severe illness is possible.
conditionalExam.diagnostic_target: Shock, hypoxemia, poor perfusion, myocarditis, PE, sepsis, or high-acuity cardiopulmonary illness.

source.source_section: physical exam cardiovascular risk factors including cyanosis, hypotension, and decreased pulses
source.implementation_notes: This is an exam maneuver; blood pressure and oxygen saturation remain baseline safety data.

conditionalExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available; abnormal perfusion findings change acuity and escalation.
conditionalExam.management_change: Cyanosis, pallor, mottling, or diaphoresis changes monitoring, oxygen, ECG/labs, senior review, and transfer urgency.
conditionalExam.limitations: Lighting, temperature, skin pigmentation, and anxiety can affect interpretation; use with vitals and perfusion.

conditionalExam.label: Palpate liver edge
conditionalExam.technique: Palpate for hepatomegaly when heart failure, myocarditis, cardiomyopathy, or pulmonary hypertension concern is present.
conditionalExam.when_to_perform: Perform when myocarditis/cardiomyopathy/heart-failure concern exists, especially with dyspnea, orthopnea, gallop, edema, or poor perfusion.
conditionalExam.diagnostic_target: Heart failure physiology, cardiomyopathy, myocarditis, or pulmonary hypertension.

source.source_section: cardiac and respiratory risk factors for pediatric chest pain
source.implementation_notes: Use as a targeted maneuver; do not include as routine bundled volume-status exam.

conditionalExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available; hepatomegaly is a supportive finding in heart-failure context.
conditionalExam.management_change: Hepatomegaly with cardiopulmonary symptoms changes ECG/labs/echo discussion, monitoring, and cardiology/senior review.
conditionalExam.limitations: Liver edge palpation varies by age/body habitus and does not confirm myocarditis or heart failure alone.

conditionalExam.label: Inspect aortopathy features
conditionalExam.technique: Inspect for tall habitus, long limbs/fingers, pectus deformity, scoliosis, hypermobility, or known syndromic/aortopathy features when history suggests connective tissue disease.
conditionalExam.when_to_perform: Perform when connective tissue disease, aortopathy, Turner syndrome, severe chest pain, or radiation to back is present.
conditionalExam.diagnostic_target: Aortopathy or connective tissue disease context that changes senior/cardiology and imaging decisions.

source.source_section: non-cardiac past medical history risk factors including aortopathy and connective tissue disease
source.implementation_notes: This is a screening maneuver; known aortopathy requires senior/cardiology guidance.

conditionalExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available; aortopathy features change risk framing rather than confirming a diagnosis.
conditionalExam.management_change: Aortopathy features change cardiology/senior review, imaging discussion, activity restriction, and discharge safety.
conditionalExam.limitations: Absence of visible features does not exclude aortopathy; known history or family history takes priority.

redFlags.label: Exertional chest pain or syncope
redFlags.action: Escalate for senior clinician and cardiology review when chest pain or syncope occurs during exercise, without prodrome, or with palpitations.
redFlags.rationale: Exertional chest pain/syncope and syncope with palpitations are pediatric cardiac red flags even when serious cardiac disease is rare.
redFlags.diagnostic_target: Cardiogenic syncope, arrhythmia, inherited arrhythmia syndrome, cardiomyopathy, myocarditis, or structural heart disease.

source.source_section: cardiogenic syncope features and consultation criteria
source.implementation_notes: Urgently refer when cardiac syncope is suspected from history, exam, or ECG.

redFlags.management_change: Changes ECG/monitoring urgency, cardiology or pediatric consultation, transfer threshold, activity restriction, and discharge eligibility.
redFlags.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to this danger trigger; guideline red flags drive escalation.
redFlags.limitations: A normal initial ECG does not make exertional syncope safe for routine discharge without clinical review.

redFlags.label: Abnormal cardiac exam or poor perfusion
redFlags.action: Escalate when cyanosis, hypotension, irregular rhythm, new/significant murmur, gallop, friction rub, muffled heart sounds, abnormal second heart sound, weak pulses, or decreased femoral pulses are present.
redFlags.rationale: Abnormal cardiac exam or perfusion findings change pediatric chest pain from reassurance to ECG, monitoring, and senior/cardiology review.
redFlags.diagnostic_target: Arrhythmia, myocarditis/pericarditis, cardiomyopathy, structural heart disease, shock, or aortopathy/perfusion disorder.

source.source_section: physical exam risk factors for cardiovascular etiology
source.implementation_notes: Discuss CXR/labs with cardiology when abnormal ECG or risk factors exist.

redFlags.management_change: Changes ECG, monitoring, CXR/labs discussion, cardiology consultation, observation, and transfer.
redFlags.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to this exam danger trigger; source-backed red flags determine action.
redFlags.limitations: Exam can be limited by noise/distress; repeat or escalate when concern remains.

redFlags.label: Family history of early sudden death or cardiomyopathy
redFlags.action: Escalate for senior/cardiology review when family history includes sudden death, unexplained sudden death in a young relative, cardiomyopathy, arrhythmia, or early myocardial infarction.
redFlags.rationale: Family history changes suspicion for inherited arrhythmia/cardiomyopathy and should not be ignored in pediatric chest pain or syncope.
redFlags.diagnostic_target: Inherited arrhythmia syndrome, cardiomyopathy, sudden cardiac death risk, or familial structural disease.

source.source_section: family history risk factors for cardiovascular etiology
source.implementation_notes: Use pediatric ECG guidance and cardiology review even when symptoms are intermittent.

redFlags.management_change: Changes ECG review, cardiology follow-up/consultation, activity restriction, family counseling, and discharge safety.
redFlags.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to this family-history trigger; guideline pathway treats it as management-changing.
redFlags.limitations: Family history may be incomplete; ask directly and update when new information appears.

redFlags.label: Hypoxia respiratory distress or hemoptysis
redFlags.action: Escalate when hypoxia, cyanosis, significant dyspnea, increased work of breathing, asymmetric/decreased breath sounds, hemoptysis, or trauma is present.
redFlags.rationale: Respiratory distress and PE/pneumothorax clues change pediatric chest pain to urgent oxygen, imaging, and respiratory/PE pathway decisions.
redFlags.diagnostic_target: Pneumonia, asthma, pneumothorax, PE, trauma-related injury, or hypoxemic cardiopulmonary illness.

source.source_section: CXR indications and PE-risk lab-testing context
source.implementation_notes: CXR is not routine; use it when fever/cough/dyspnea/hypoxia/abnormal breath sounds/trauma or abnormal heart sounds support it.

redFlags.management_change: Changes oxygen, CXR, respiratory treatment, PE add-on, monitoring, and transfer threshold.
redFlags.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to this respiratory danger trigger; source-specific testing is indicated.
redFlags.limitations: Normal CXR can occur early in some pathology; persistent hypoxia or distress requires reassessment.

redFlags.label: Abnormal pediatric ECG
redFlags.action: Escalate abnormal ECG findings to senior clinician and cardiology review; high-risk findings include long QT, WPW, heart block, frequent PVCs, ventricular bigeminy/trigeminy, or significant ST elevation.
redFlags.rationale: Pediatric ECG characteristics vary with age and abnormal findings can indicate life-threatening arrhythmia, myocarditis/pericarditis, or structural disease.
redFlags.diagnostic_target: Long QT syndrome, WPW, conduction disease, ventricular arrhythmia, myocarditis/pericarditis, or ischemic/inflammatory myocardial injury.

source.source_section: ECG indications, interpretation principles, QT interval, and cardiac red flags
source.implementation_notes: Do not rely solely on automated ECG intervals; manual calculation and clinical context are required.

redFlags.management_change: Changes monitoring, cardiology consultation, repeat ECG/manual interval review, echo/Holter/exercise-test consideration, admission, and activity restriction.
redFlags.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to abnormal ECG escalation; ECG findings and clinical context drive action.
redFlags.limitations: Lead misplacement and machine interpretation can mislead; repeat or manually review when findings do not fit the clinical picture.

initialTests.label: ECG when cardiac risk or syncope is present
initialTests.action: Obtain ECG when cardiac cause cannot be excluded by history/exam, when syncope is present at least once, or when palpitations/exertional symptoms/family history/abnormal exam are present.
initialTests.rationale: ECG is a key pediatric test for chest pain with risk factors and for syncope; it should be interpreted with age-specific limits and clinical context.
initialTests.diagnostic_target: Arrhythmia, long QT, WPW, conduction disease, myocarditis/pericarditis, cardiomyopathy clue, or cardiac syncope.

source.source_section: common ECG indications and red-flag ECG review principles
source.implementation_notes: Check lead placement and manually review intervals when automated interpretation is discordant.

initialTests.management_change: Abnormal ECG changes monitoring, cardiology consultation, admission/transfer, echo/Holter/exercise testing, and activity restriction.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: Guideline pathways do not provide one ECG LR for pediatric chest pain; ECG is indication- and finding-driven.
initialTests.limitations: Normal ECG does not exclude all cardiac disease, especially when exertional syncope, family history, or abnormal exam persists.

initialTests.label: Chest X-ray only when indicated
initialTests.action: Order CXR when fever, cough, shortness of breath, hypoxia, abnormal or asymmetric breath sounds, trauma, worsening murmur, muffled heart sounds, or friction rub is present; avoid routine CXR in low-risk chest pain.
initialTests.rationale: CXR is not mandatory for pediatric chest pain and should be targeted to respiratory, trauma, or selected cardiac/pericardial clues.
initialTests.diagnostic_target: Pneumonia, pneumothorax, trauma-related injury, cardiomegaly/pericardial concern, or respiratory chest pain.

source.source_section: chest radiograph indications and non-routine use in pediatric chest pain
source.implementation_notes: Use source-specific respiratory findings; do not use CXR as a blanket default.

initialTests.management_change: Abnormal CXR changes antibiotics, bronchodilator/oxygen care, pneumothorax management, cardiology discussion, observation, and transfer.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not attached to CXR selection in this pathway; testing is indication-driven.
initialTests.limitations: A normal CXR does not exclude early pneumonia, PE, myocarditis, or arrhythmia.

initialTests.label: Troponin and inflammatory labs when indicated
initialTests.action: Use troponin and selected CBC/ESR/CRP/blood cultures only when history, exam, ECG, or CXR suggests myocardial ischemia, myocarditis/pericarditis, or infectious/post-infectious cause.
initialTests.rationale: Routine labs are not recommended for all pediatric chest pain; targeted labs change myocarditis/pericarditis, ischemia, or infection management.
initialTests.diagnostic_target: Myocarditis, pericarditis, myocardial ischemia/injury, infectious endocarditis, or post-infectious inflammatory chest pain.

source.source_section: lab testing indications and troponin use in pediatric chest pain
source.implementation_notes: Troponin is typically considered with abnormal ECG or crushing substernal pain radiating to back/neck, not as routine screening for all children.

initialTests.management_change: Positive troponin or inflammatory/infectious labs change cardiology/senior consultation, admission, imaging, antimicrobials when indicated, and activity restriction.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not attached to routine lab selection; guideline pathways recommend labs only when clinical/ECG/CXR context supports them.
initialTests.limitations: Troponin interpretation depends on assay and clinical context; a negative troponin alone does not clear concerning history or ECG.

initialTests.label: Syncope ECG glucose anemia and pregnancy tests
initialTests.action: For syncope, obtain ECG at least once, check bedside glucose if seen shortly after the event, obtain CBC if anemia is suspected, and consider pregnancy testing when applicable.
initialTests.rationale: Syncope testing should target cardiac red flags and common mimics rather than broad untailored workups.
initialTests.diagnostic_target: Cardiac syncope, hypoglycemia, anemia/menorrhagia, pregnancy-related syncope, or seizure mimic.

source.source_section: syncope investigations
source.implementation_notes: Use adolescent confidential care and local pregnancy testing policy when pregnancy is possible.

initialTests.management_change: Abnormal ECG/glucose/CBC/pregnancy testing changes treatment, specialty referral, observation, and discharge safety.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not assigned to this targeted syncope test bundle; guideline indications and results change management.
initialTests.limitations: Do not use normal glucose or CBC to dismiss cardiac syncope red flags.

initialTests.label: Echo Holter or exercise testing after review
initialTests.action: Use echocardiogram, ambulatory rhythm monitoring, or exercise tolerance testing only after senior clinician/cardiology review when red flags, abnormal ECG, or persistent concern exists.
initialTests.rationale: Advanced cardiac tests are management-changing but should be selected by risk features and specialist review rather than automatic pediatric chest pain screening.
initialTests.diagnostic_target: Structural heart disease, cardiomyopathy, myocarditis/pericarditis complication, exertional arrhythmia, long QT/WPW, or inherited cardiac disease.

source.source_section: red flags needing senior review and possible further investigation including 24-hour ECG, echocardiogram, or exercise tolerance test
source.implementation_notes: Do not generate automatic echo/Holter/stress testing recommendations for low-risk reproducible chest wall pain.

initialTests.management_change: Findings change cardiology ownership, admission/transfer, activity restriction, medication/procedure planning, and family screening.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not assigned; advanced testing is selected after risk review and ECG/exam context.
initialTests.limitations: Advanced testing access and thresholds vary by institution; use local cardiology pathway.

dispositionRules.label: Emergency pediatric cardiology escalation
dispositionRules.action: Escalate urgently for exertional chest pain/syncope, abnormal cardiac exam, abnormal ECG, persistent symptoms, family sudden-death/cardiomyopathy history, or suspected myocarditis/pericarditis/arrhythmia.
dispositionRules.rationale: These features define the management-changing minority of pediatric chest pain/syncope cases that should not be treated as benign or adult ACS low risk.
dispositionRules.diagnostic_target: Cardiogenic syncope, arrhythmia, myocarditis/pericarditis, cardiomyopathy, structural disease, or inherited cardiac risk.

source.source_section: cardiac red flags and abnormal ECG review
source.implementation_notes: Discuss cardiac red flags with senior clinician even when an ECG appears normal.

dispositionRules.management_change: Changes monitoring, cardiology/senior consultation, admission/transfer, advanced testing, and activity restriction.
dispositionRules.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to escalation disposition; source-backed red flags determine urgency.
dispositionRules.limitations: Local cardiology availability and transfer pathways vary; do not delay stabilization while arranging consultation.

dispositionRules.label: Low-risk chest pain testing restraint
dispositionRules.action: When red flags are absent, vitals/exam are reassuring, pain is reproducible or consistent with precordial catch/costochondritis, and follow-up is reliable, avoid routine ECG/CXR/labs and provide analgesia/reassurance/safety net as appropriate.
dispositionRules.rationale: Most pediatric chest pain is self-limited; unnecessary testing can create false positives, cost, radiation, and anxiety.
dispositionRules.diagnostic_target: Low-risk musculoskeletal chest pain, precordial catch, anxiety, or reflux pattern after red flags are denied.

source.source_section: key points, investigations reserved for risk factors, and reassurance guidance
source.implementation_notes: Low-risk disposition requires documented absence of cardiac/respiratory/syncope/family-history red flags.

dispositionRules.management_change: Changes from broad testing to analgesia/reassurance, return precautions, activity advice, and outpatient follow-up.
dispositionRules.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to testing restraint; disposition depends on active red-flag denial and exam context.
dispositionRules.limitations: Do not apply when exertional symptoms, syncope, palpitations, abnormal vitals, abnormal exam, family history, or abnormal ECG is present.

dispositionRules.label: Syncope consultation and discharge route
dispositionRules.action: For syncope, consult pediatrics/cardiology when cardiac syncope is suspected from history, exam, or ECG; consult neurology for seizure-suspicious presentations; discharge only when likely cause is identified and follow-up is arranged.
dispositionRules.rationale: Syncope disposition depends on cause identification, ECG/exam risk, persistent symptoms, seizure mimic, and local capability.
dispositionRules.diagnostic_target: Cardiac syncope, recurrent vasovagal/orthostatic syncope, seizure mimic, persistent symptoms, or low-risk vasovagal syncope.

source.source_section: consultation, transfer, and discharge criteria
source.implementation_notes: Transfer when persistently symptomatic, clustering with cardiac features, or care exceeds local comfort.

dispositionRules.management_change: Changes specialty referral, transfer, fluids/salt education, trigger avoidance, seizure evaluation, and follow-up.
dispositionRules.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to syncope disposition; history/exam/ECG and persistence determine route.
dispositionRules.limitations: Frequent or problematic vasovagal/orthostatic syncope that does not respond to nonpharmacologic treatment still needs follow-up.

dispositionRules.label: Respiratory or PE route
dispositionRules.action: When hypoxia, dyspnea, hemoptysis, pleuritic pain with PE risk, abnormal breath sounds, trauma, or pneumothorax concern is present, add pediatric respiratory/PE evaluation rather than relying on adult ACS-only workup.
dispositionRules.rationale: Pediatric chest pain red flags often route to respiratory, PE, or trauma pathways rather than adult ACS scores.
dispositionRules.diagnostic_target: Pneumonia, asthma, pneumothorax, PE, trauma, or hypoxemic illness.

source.source_section: CXR and lab/PE testing indications
source.implementation_notes: Use local pediatric PE pathway for D-dimer/imaging thresholds and anticoagulation decisions.

dispositionRules.management_change: Changes oxygen, imaging, respiratory treatment, PE/DVT secondary workup, anticoagulation safety review, and transfer.
dispositionRules.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to respiratory/PE route selection; use condition-specific pathways.
dispositionRules.limitations: Pediatric PE is uncommon but high risk; do not dismiss risk-factor-positive hypoxic or pleuritic presentations.

dispositionRules.label: Activity restriction and return precautions
dispositionRules.action: Restrict strenuous activity and provide urgent return precautions when exertional symptoms, syncope, palpitations, abnormal ECG/exam, myocarditis/pericarditis concern, or unresolved cardiac risk exists.
dispositionRules.rationale: Pediatric cardiac red flags can recur during exercise and need explicit safety instructions before outpatient follow-up.
dispositionRules.diagnostic_target: Unresolved cardiac red flag, exertional symptoms, arrhythmia risk, myocarditis/pericarditis, or cardiogenic syncope concern.

source.source_section: cardiac red flags needing senior review and further investigation
source.implementation_notes: Pair with local sports/activity clearance rules and cardiology follow-up.

dispositionRules.management_change: Changes sports/activity clearance, outpatient cardiology follow-up, return precautions, and family counseling.
dispositionRules.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to safety-net disposition; red flags and unresolved concern determine restrictions.
dispositionRules.limitations: This module does not replace sport-specific clearance or cardiology advice for known heart disease.

guideline_cutoffs.label: Pediatric syncope: obtain an ECG at least once; urgent referral for exertional syncope, chest pain/palpitations, abnormal ECG, or family history
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: An ECG should be obtained in all children with syncope at least once; cardiac syncope concern includes exercise association, no prodrome, chest pain/palpitations, past cardiac disease, or family history of early cardiac death/arrhythmia/sudden death.

guideline_cutoffs.label: Pediatric ECG high-risk cutoffs: QRS >0.12 sec, QTc >450 ms or <=340 ms, ST shift >2 mm in precordial leads
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: Pediatric ECG review should flag QRS duration >0.12 seconds, manual QTc outside >340 ms and <=450 ms, or ST elevation/depression above 2 mm in precordial leads as abnormal/high-risk in context.
